text: From BBC Learning English.

This is Learning English from the News,
our podcast about the news headlines.
In this programme,

Bad Bunny's Super Bowl performance
and what people are saying about it.
Hello, I'm Pippa.
And I'm Becca.

In this programme,
we look at one big story

and the vocabulary in the headlines
that will help you understand it.

You can find all the vocabulary
and headlines from this episode,

as well as a worksheet
on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
So, let's hear more about this story.

Benito Antonio Martínez Ocasio,
who is the singer known as Bad Bunny,
gave a controversial performance

at the Super Bowl halftime show
last weekend.

His performance was like
no other seen in Super Bowl history
because it was entirely in Spanish.

Yes, the 14-minute set, which invited
celebrities to dance alongside him,

had only one line in English,
which was "God Bless America."

This line was said just before naming
multiple countries and territories,

including his home country,
Puerto Rico,

with dancers carrying their flags
in celebration.

The BBC described it as "one big love
letter to his native Puerto Rico."

A billboard message was shown towards
the end that read,

"The only thing more powerful
than hate is love."

Bad Bunny than held an American
football with the slogan in English,
"Together we are America."

But US President Donald Trump called
the performance "an affront

to the greatness of America",
meaning he thought it was offensive.

Other American Republicans
were also critical.
Let's have our first headline.
This is from BBC News.

Bad Bunny makes history as Trump
criticises 'terrible' Super Bowl show.
That headline again from BBC News.

Bad Bunny makes history as Trump
criticises 'terrible' Super Bowl show.

This headline mentions how Bad
Bunny's performance made history.

What can you tell us
about this phrase, Pippa?

Well, this phrase, 'makes history' is
a common expression,

which means to do something
that hasn't been done before.

Bad Bunny's performance made history
because it was

the first ever Super Bowl performance
in Spanish.

It is likely to be remembered
for a long time.

Right, and the performance
also made history

because a young couple got married
in the crowd around the stage
as part of the show.

Yes, the first
of its kind at the Super Bowl.

We've had make history do something
that hasn't been done before.
For example,

Ada Lovelace made history when she
produced the first computer programme.

This is Learning English from the News,
our podcast about the news headlines.

Today we're talking about Bad Bunny's
historic Super Bowl performance.

Bad Bunny has recently made history
by winning a Grammy

for the Album of the Year with his
Spanish album, Debí Tirar Más Fotos.

Yes, and he was also the most streamed
artist globally on Spotify in 2025.

During his performance
at the Super Bowl,

Bad Bunny has an entire community
around him.
You can see people playing dominoes,

street vendors selling
traditional Latin American food,

and even a casita, or little house, like
the ones you might see in Puerto Rico.

Our next headline is a review
of his performance.

This is from The Guardian,
a British newspaper.

Bad Bunny's Super Bowl half-time show
review - a thrilling ode to Boricua joy.

That headline again
from The Guardian.

Bad Bunny Super Bowl half-time Show
review - a thrilling ode to Boricua joy.

This headline is about how Bad
Bunny's performance shows his pride
and joy for Puerto Rico.

We're looking at this expression
'ode to'.

Now, an ode is literally
a poem that celebrates
or expresses love for something.

But we use the expression ode
to something

to refer to anything
that celebrates or expresses love,

for example, a song, a speech,
or a letter.
Exactly. As expressed in the headline,

Bad Bunny's performance was an ode
to Puerto Rican life and culture.

We've had ode
to something, such as a work of art

or performance, that expresses
celebration or love for something.

For example, the CEO's retirement
speech was an ode to her company.

This is Learning English from
the News from BBC Learning English.
We're talking about Bad Bunny's
Super Bowl performance.

Bad Bunny appeared previously
as a guest singer for
the 2020 Super Bowl halftime show.

This year's show featured a maze
of sugarcane

where Bad Bunny encountered symbols
of Puerto Rican life,

such as salsa dancing,
coconut water sellers, and a nail salon.

In the past, Bad Bunny
has been critical of President Trump,
but he did not use his performance

at the Super Bowl to
directly criticise the US government.
Yes. As we mentioned,

Bad Bunny's performance was
a celebration of Puerto Rico,

but was described by President Trump
as "absolutely ridiculous".
Here's our next headline.

This headline is from the Financial
Times, a British business newspaper.

Bad Bunny's Super Bowl halftime
show puts pizzazz before politics -
but still irks Trump.

That headline again
from the Financial Times.

Bad Bunny's Super Bowl halftime
show puts pizzazz before politics -
but still irks Trump.

This headline includes President Trump's
reaction to Bad Bunny's performance.

It says the performance irks him,
which means annoys him.
But we're not focusing on this word.

We're going to look
at the word pizzazz.

Pizzazz means
an energetic or excitable quality.

We use pizzazz to refer
to things like performances,

or things that are glamorous
or noticeable.
We say they have pizzazz.
And let's look at how it's used.
In the headline,

it says that Bad Bunny's halftime
show put pizzazz before politics.

Could you tell us
what this means, Pippa?

Of course.
To put something

before another thing is to give it
greater priority or importance.

So, for example, I could say that I
often put work before family.

Right. So, bad Bunny put pizzazz
before politics.

He put on an exciting show rather
than focusing on politics.
Let's have a recap.

We had pizzazz,
an energetic or excitable quality.
For example, I love musicals.

The performances always have
so much pizzazz.

That's it for this episode
of Learning English from the News.

We'll be back next week
with another news story.

If you like music, you might enjoy
our series, My Song, My Home,

where we learn English
from songs around the UK.

You can find episodes
at bbclearningenglish.com

And don't forget to follow us on

Bye for now.
